_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 9-45R Verapamil (Revised 8/11/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Medication Protocol Section 9-45R: Verapamil — MDHHS Approved 8/11/2023, Reviewed 2023
indications: Narrow complex tachycardia — irregular (atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter with rapid ventricular response)
contraindications: Age <1 year (CONTRAINDICATED — may cause fatal cardiovascular collapse in infants)
Wide complex tachycardia (do NOT use — may cause decompensation in V-Tach)
WPW (Wolff-Parkinson-White) with pre-excitation — may accelerate conduction through accessory pathway
Sinus node dysfunction (sick sinus syndrome) without pacemaker
Concurrent IV beta-blocker use — high risk of asystole
precautions: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION: age <1 year — fatal cardiovascular collapse reported; do NOT use in infants
Monitor BP and ECG continuously during and after administration
Do NOT use concurrently with IV beta-blockers — risk of profound bradycardia and asystole
Administer slowly IV to minimize hypotension
expected_effects: AV node slowing and refractoriness — rate control in atrial fibrillation/flutter
notes: Adult standard dose: 5 mg IV; administer slowly over 2-3 minutes
Preferred over adenosine for recurrent SVT or rate control in atrial fibrillation
CONTRAINDICATED in patients <1 year — use adenosine for pediatric SVT instead
Diltiazem (9-21R) is an alternative calcium channel blocker with similar indications and slightly different dosing profile

dosing.population: Adult (>14 yr) — narrow complex tachycardia (regular or irregular)
dosing.notes: Monitor BP and ECG during administration; stop if hypotension; CONTRAINDICATED <1 year
